summary: Gruber argues that online ad tracking is a fundamental violation of privacy that society has tolerated only because it's invisible. Using Apple's new iPhone privacy ad as a jumping-off point, he contends that the tracking industry has no legitimate right to the data it collects, and that iOS 14's new permission dialog simply makes the reality of tracking visible in plain language. He dismisses the ad industry's complaints about Apple's changes as pure entitlement, arguing that if real-world marketers attempted equivalent surveillance, people would be horrified. The tracking industry's business model is built on betrayal of user privacy, and no action Apple takes against it is too strong.
key_points: Apple's iPhone privacy ad works because real-world equivalents of online tracking would trigger immediate alarm and rejection
The ad tracking industry led by Facebook has no legitimate right to the tracking data it has been collecting — society only tolerated it because it was invisible
iOS 14's permission dialog isn't scaring users unfairly; it's accurately describing something no sane person would consent to
The tracking industry's objections boil down to entitlement: they believe privacy is theirs because they've been taking it without consequence
The existence of a multi-billion-dollar industry built on privacy violations doesn't legitimize those violations
money_quotes: The entitlement of these fuckers is just off the charts.
They have zero right, none, to the tracking they've been getting away with.
We, as a society, have implicitly accepted it because we never really noticed it.
Our brains are naturally attuned to detect and viscerally reject, with outrage and alarm, real-world intrusions into our privacy.
Apple's tracking permission dialog is something no sane person would agree to because this sort of tracking is something no sane person would agree to.

Just because there is now a multi-billion-dollar industry based on the abject betrayal of our privacy doesn't mean the sociopaths who built it have any right whatsoever to continue getting away with it.
No action Apple can take against the tracking industry is too strong.
Their argument boils down to entitlement: they think our privacy is theirs for the taking because they've been getting away with taking it without our knowledge, and it is valuable.
key_insight: Online ad tracking has survived only because it's invisible — making it visible through honest permission dialogs reveals it as something no rational person would consent to.
